fr:mainmatter.html:p: This week, we saw one way to provide models for intuitionistic logic. Each of these models has two components: a frame, and a valuation on that frame. You can think of a frame as a bunch of points with some collection of arrows between them; all that really matters is which of these points are reachable from which. A valuation on a frame assigns some set of atomic formulas to each point in the frame, subject to the condition that when x R y, the set assigned to x must be a subset of the set assigned to y. Given a model, we can calculate a value for every sentence at every point in the model.

A counterexample to an argument is a model containing a point at which all the premises get the value Y but the conclusion gets the value ?. Using this notion of counterexample with these models, we arrive at intuitionistic logic. We have also looked at some stronger logics that we can arrive at by considering certain special kinds of these models.
This week, we'll return to thinking about models. We'll look at the most usual models used for intuitionistic logic, which is a logic we haven't so far seen through a model-theoretic lens.

Remember that when we're thinking about a logic model-theoretically, the central notion we use is the idea of counterexamples. We need to say what our models are, and we need to say what it takes for a model to be a counterexample to an argument. Then the arguments we count as valid are the ones that have no counterexamples.

In the first half of the unit, the models we looked at were valuations in structures. It is possible to make models for intuitionistic logic that way, but it's a pain in the rear. Intuitionistic logic is a matrix logic, so it is possible to give a single matrix that determines intuitionistic logic perfectly. But the structure that matrix is built on is infinitely large; it has infinitely many truth values. So it's not like our simple three- and four-valued critters from earlier in the semester.

Because of this, we'll use a different kind of model altogether, not involving structures at all. The keys are:
what a model is, and
what it takes to for one of them to be a counterexample to an argument.
That much is familiar at this point. But our models for intuitionistic logic will not be valuations in structures, so much will also be different!

The models we'll use for intuitionistic logic have two components: a frame and a valuation on that frame. (As we'll see, these valuations are different from the kinds of valuations in structures we looked at earlier in the semester.)
First, though, frames. The easiest way to think of frames, I think, is as a bunch of points with some collection of arrows between them. For example, this is a frame:

For later reference, let's name this frame "`Example frame 1"'. In Example frame 1, I've named the points 1, 2, 3, 4; in general I will use numbers for points. As so often when we use numbers, though, their numeric properties don't matter; it never matters which number we use for what, and we'll never add them or anything. I'm just using numbers as a convenient stock of tags.

In a frame, you can connect points with arrows any way you like. This could include no connections at all, or every point connected to every other point, or some selection in between, as we see above.

There are only two things we need to keep track of about a frame: what its points are, and which points are reachable from which points. To think about reaching a point x from a point y, imagine that you are standing at y and trying to get to x, and that you can only travel along arrows, and only in the direction that the arrows indicate. If it is possible to get from y to x in this way, then y can reach x, and we write y R x to have a quick way to say this. We can also say that x is reachable from y to mean the same thing. You don't have to move at all if you don't need to, so every point is automatically reachable from itself.

For example, in Example frame 1, all of the following reachability claims are true, and no others:
1 R 1, and 2 R 2, and 3 R 3, and 4 R 4
1 R 2
1 R 3
2 R 3
3 R 2

And that's all we need to know about Example frame 1. Note that we have 1 R 3 here, even though there is no arrow in the diagram directly from 1 to 3. We can still reach 3 from 1 along the arrows, by first stepping to 2 and then to 3. (Or by doing that and then stepping around the loop connecting 2 and 3 any number of times.)

In fact, since every point is reachable from itself, we don't even really need to keep track of the points in a frame separately; if we know what's reachable from what, we will automatically have a list of all the points.
Any way of setting up points and arrows determines some story about reachability among those points, so any way of setting up points and arrows gives us a frame.

You might note that, if we added to the diagram of Example frame 1 a new arrow directly from 1 to 3, that wouldn't have any effect on what's reachable from what, since we already have 1 R 3 in this frame. Since reachability is all that matters to a frame, if we're being picky we'd say that the points-and-arrows depiction I've given you isn't Example frame 1 itself, but rather a presentation of Example frame 1; and adding an arrow from 1 to 3 would give us a different presentation of the very same frame. For our purposes, though, we don't need to be this picky; points-and-arrows gives a nice way to think about frames, as long as you remember that all that really matters is reachability.

A frame is some set of things, called points, with some arrows between them, in any configuration. (We usually use numbers for points, but that's not very important; it's just convenient.)
All that matters about a frame is which points are reachable from which points. Every point can reach itself, plus points can reach along any arrows in the frame, and they can follow chains of arrows: anywhere that a particular point can reach in this way is reachable from that starting point. (Any reaching along an arrow or along a chain of arrows must follow the direction of all the arrows in question.)

Example frame 1 is finite, and of course any frame that I can depict like this will be finite. But there is no restriction in general to finite frames; a frame can certainly contain infinitely many points if you like. As long as it's clearly specified what's reachable from what, there's no problem with this.

If you're interested in the details: what matters about reachability here is just that it's a preorder. I won't define "preorder" here, since it's not a concept we'll need. But if you already know what a preorder is, or if you feel like looking it up, that's exactly what we're using here. Using points and arrows, and then thinking about reachability via those arrows, is just a hopefully-at-least-somewhat-intuitive way of getting at preorders.
That's all pretty abstract so far, and it's at that abstract level that logic really lives and breathes. It can sometimes help to have a more concrete way of thinking about things, though.

One way these models are sometimes thought of is as representing a kind of temporal growth of knowledge. In this way of thinking, each point in a model represents a particular stage of knowledge, and as we move around a frame, along the given paths, we get to points that have more and more knowledge. On this way of thinking about models, when x and y are points in a model, and x R y, that means something like: if we're at stage x of knowledge, there is something we could learn, without forgetting anything, that would move us to stage y.

On the other hand, when it's not the case that x R y, that means something like: if we're at stage x of knowledge, there's no way we could learn any more than we already know and end up at y. Since y doesn't preserve everything we already have at x, we would have to forget something to get from x to y.

On this interpretation, we're thinking about knowledge as something that can never be lost. (Or at least we're not thinking about what losing it would be like.) We're only concerned with which sets are subsets of which others: which can be extended without losing anything to reach which others. That's probably not a great theory of how knowledge actually works! (For example, I know where my keys are right now; but maybe tomorrow I won't.) So don't get too distracted by this kind of interpretation; it's just here to give the flavour. If you can imagine a kind of cumulative development of knowledge, that never loses anything as it goes, that's the kind of picture. Other interpretations of frames are of course possible, but hopefully this helps sink in an image.

Now, once we have a frame, to reach a model we need a valuation on that frame. These valuations are like our valuations on structures in some ways but not in others. What will be familiar is that these valuations start by assigning values to atomic formulas, and work up from there in specified ways to assign values to compound formulas. But the way this works will be new.

We work with two values in these models, which I'll call Y and ?. I've chosen these names to suggest the knowledge interpretaion explained above: roughly, something gets the value Y when it's known (Yes), and the value ? when it's not known. It's ? and not something like N because when something is not known, it might later come to be known or it might not; it's not yet a No, just a ?.

As I mentioned above, what's known or not might vary between points in a model. So different sentences can get different values at different points in a model. When we're keeping track of assigning these values to sentences, then, we always need to have in mind both a particular model and a particular point in that model. To indicate the value that a sentence A takes at the point x in the model M, I write M(A, x). So if A takes the value Y there, then M(A, x) = Y.

These valuations on a frame start from assigning values to atomic formulas, and then on this basis we can calculate the values assigned to compound formulas. We do this by assigning to each point in the frame some set of atomic formulas, to be understood as the set of atomic formulas that get the value Y at that point.

But unlike when we're dealing with valuations on structures, here the assignment of values to atomic formulas is not completely unconstrained. Recall the knowledge interpretation: when x R y in a frame, we want that to mean that we can move from x to y without losing any knowledge that's present at x. So our valuations are constrained in this way: if M(p, x) = Y, and x R y in M, then we must have M(p, y) = Y as well. That goes for any atomic sentence, not just p. In other words, when x R y in a frame, then the set of atomic formulas a valuation assigns to x must be a subset of the set it assigns to y.

A valuation on a frame is a choice of a set of atomic sentences for each point in the frame, subject to the condition that for all points x, y in the frame, if x R y, then the set assigned to x must be a subset of the set assigned to y.

For an example, return to Example frame 1. Let's assign sets of atomics to points as follows: 1 gets \{p, q\}; 2 gets \{p, q, r\}; 3 gets \{p, q, r\}; and 4 gets \{q, s\}. This meets our requirement: whenever one point is reachable from another in this frame, the point we're reaching from gets a subset of the set assigned to the point we're reaching to. Note as well that when we have a loop of reachability, like between 2 and 3 in this frame, then that means that every point in the loop must be assigned the very same set of atomics.

In this model, I've assigned only a finite set of atomics to each point. But this is not required at all; any choice of sets of atomics is a valuation, so long as it connects to reachability in the required way.

That's what an intuitionistic model is: a frame, plus a set of atomics chosen for each point in the frame, such that whenever x R y in the frame, the set assigned to x is a subset of the set assigned to y.
An intuitionistic model is a frame, plus a valuation on that frame.
On this basis, we can calculate values for every formula of our language at every point in the model. Here's how.

As before, for any atomic sentence A, we will have M(A, x) = Y if A is a member of the set assigned to the point x in the model M. For compound sentences, we proceed by way of the main connective of the sentence. I'll discuss \land , \lor , \top , \bot first, since those are simpler; I'll put off \neg , \rightarrow for now.

As you can see, this is just like the Boolean structure, but with Y playing the role of \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and ? the role of \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. The difference of the names of the values isn't really important; it's just there to be reminiscent of a difference in how these values are often interpreted. For example, where our clause for conjunction in the classical case is often taken to indicate that a conjunction is true iff both conjuncts are true, here the corresponding clause is meant to indicate that a conjunction is known iff both conjuncts are known.

This should lead you to wonder about disjunction: surely it's possible to know a disjunction without knowing either disjunct, but the clause we have here seems to rule this out. In fact, there is another way to proceed that would be more careful about disjunction, for just this reason; we're working this way because it's simpler, even though it strains the knowledge interpretation. Our method is based on what are sometimes called "Kripke models"; the more involved method would work with what are called "Beth models". Both kinds of model determine intuitionistic logic.

If that were all there was to it, though, then there would be no point in bundling up multiple points into a frame. Where that stuff comes into play is with the remaining connectives: \neg and \rightarrow . To give a value to \neg A at a point x in a model, we don't just look at x itself. Instead, we look at every point that's reachable from x in the model. Remember, every point is reachable from itself; so this includes looking at x. But it often includes more.

For \neg A to get the value Y at a point x, what's required is that at every point reachable from x in the model, A gets the value ?. The idea is that it's not enough in general for us to know \neg A just that we don't yet know A. Something more is needed. What's needed is that there is no way to extend our knowledge where we could come to know A. Or at least, that's the idea that these models go with.

The idea for implication is similar. Again, to give a value to A \rightarrow B at a point x in a model M, we look at every point reachable from x in M. For A \rightarrow B to get the value Y, what's required is this: that there's no way to extend our knowledge to know A without also knowing B. That is, we require that every point y reachable from x where M(A, y)= Y also gives us M(B, y) = Y.
Let's look at some examples, using a new example model, which I'l call M:

Here, I've indicated the valuation along with the frame, by putting at each point the set of atomic sentences assigned to that point. Here are some example calculations of values of various compound sentences at points in M:
M(\neg q, 5) = ?. Even though we have M(q, 5) = ?, the fact that M(q, 3) = Y, together with the fact that 5 R 3, forces this result.
M(\neg u, 2) = Y. Here, we have two points we need to check: 2 and 1. Since both of these points assign the value ? to the atomic sentence u, we have this result.
M(q \rightarrow r, 5) = ?. Since we have M(q, 3) = Y and M(r, 3) = ?, this result follows.
M(r \rightarrow q, 5) = ?. Since we have M(r, 4) = Y and M(q, 4) = ?, this result follows.
M((q \rightarrow r) \lor (r \rightarrow q), 5) = ?. We've seen that both of the components of this disjunction take value ? at 5, so the disjunction does as well.
Note what's happened here: we've got a reflection at 5 of what's going on at both 3 and 4: in particular, 3 gives us q without r and 4 gives us r without q. Our connectives are giving us a way to "look around" inside the model, in a limited way.
M((q \land r) \rightarrow s, 5) = Y. Here, we need to check every point, since every point is reachable from 5. What we see is that there are only two points that give the value Y\ to q \land r; these are 2 and 1. And both of these points also give the value Y to s. So we have the result.
M((q \land r) \rightarrow t, 5) = ?. Again, we check every point, and see that just 2 and 1 give the value Y\ to q \land r. This time, though, one of them (viz 2) gives ? to the consequent t. So we get this result instead.
M((q \land r) \rightarrow t, 2) = ?. This time we only need to look at 2 and 1, but since 2 still gives Y to the antecedent and ? to the consequent, it's the same result.
M((q \land r) \rightarrow t, 1) = Y. Here, we only need to look at 1 itself, since 1 is the only point reachable from 1 in M. And here, we have the value Y for both p \land q and t. So we get this result.
M(\neg ((q \land r) \rightarrow t), 2) = ?. Here, we look at 2 and 1, since these are the points reachable from 2 in M. At each of these, we ask: what is the value of (q \land r) \rightarrow t here? As we've seen, it's Y at 1 and ? at 2. So we have this result.
Let's wrap all this up in a definition for later reference:

Given an intuitionistic model M and a point x in it, we assign values to every sentence at x in the model as follows:
atomic: for any atomic sentence A, we have M(A, x) = Y iff A is in the set of atomic sentences assigned to x by the model, and otherwise M(A, x) = ?.
conjunction: for any sentences A and B, we have M(A \land B, x) = Y iff M(A, x) = M(B, x) = Y, and otherwise M(A \land B, x) = ?.
disjunction: for any sentences A and B, we have M(A \lor B, x) = ? iff M(A, x) = M(B, x) = ?, and otherwise M(A \lor B, x) = Y.
verum and falsum: always, M(\top , x) = Y and M(\bot , x) = vQ.
negation: for any sentence A, we have M(\neg A, x) = Y iff every point y reachable from x is such that M(A, y) = vQ; otherwise, M(\neg A, x) = vQ.
implication: for any sentences A and B, we have M(A \rightarrow B, x) = vQ iff there is some point y reachable from x with M(A, y) = Y and M(B, y) = ?; otherwise M(A \rightarrow B, x) = Y.

We can also do some more general reasoning about possible values at points. The most important thing to notice here is called persistence: in any model N, at any points x and y, if x R y and N(A, x) = Y, then N(A, y) = Y as well. That is, as our knowledge of atomic sentences grows, moving along the paths in our frame, the same thing happens with compound sentences as well. We never lose knowledge of any sentence, atomic or compound, as long as we move only to reachable points. For atomic formulas, we have forced this directly, with our restrictions on valuations. But for compound sentences, this is a more complex point; it's true because of the interactions between the various connectives.

To see that persistence is true, we can bring back our old friend induction. We want to show that persistence holds for absolutely every sentence A. Recall that induction has two main parts: first, we show that persistence holds for atomic sentences. Then, we go connective by connective, showing that for any compound sentence, as long as persistence holds of its components, then it holds of the compound sentence itself. That's enough to show that persistence holds for every sentence. We'd have no way to build a sentence where it failed, since we'd have to build the sentence ultimately from atomics using connectives.

So first, let's look at persistence for atomics. This says that in any model N, for any points x and y and any atomic sentence A, if x R y and N(A, x) = Y, then N(A, y) = Y as well. Here, though, this is required as we specify a valuation; any way to make this fail doesn't count as a valuation at all. That is, persistence does indeed hold for atomics. That's the first part done!
On to the connectives.

html:ul.html:li: For a conjunction A \land B: we need to show that A \land B is persistent, as long as A and B are. So we assume that A and B are persistent, since if they're not we don't need to worry about A \land B. Suppose, then, that x R y in some model N, and that N(A \land B, x) = Y. By the way values are assigned to conjunctions, this means that both A and B also get the value Y at x in N. And since both A and B are persistent, they both must get the value Y at y in N as well. But then N(A \land B, y) = Y, and this is all we need.

For a disjunction A \lor B, the reasoning is very similar to conjunction. Again, we assume that A and B are persistent, since if they're not we don't need to worry about A \lor B. Suppose, then, that x R y in some model N, and that N(A \lor B, x) = Y. By the way values are assigned to disjunctions, this means at least one of A and B also gets the value Y at x in N. And since both A and B are persistent, at least one of them must get the value Y at y in N as well. But then N(A \lor B, y) = Y, and this is all we need.
Showing that \bot is persistent is quick: it never takes the value Y anywhere, so we're all set!
Similarly for \top ; since it takes the value Y everywhere, we're good!

For a negation 
eg A: we need to show that 
eg A is persistent as long as A is. So suppose A is persistent, and that x R y in some model N with N(
eg A, x) = Y. That means that in every point reachable from x in N, the sentence A gets the value ?. Now, since y is itself reachable from x, every place reachable from y is also reachable from x---go from x to y first, and then on to wherever. So in all these points reachable from y, the sentence A gets the value ?. And that's enough for it to be the case that N(
eg A, y) = Y.

Finally, implication: we need to show that A \rightarrow B is persistent as long as A and B are. So suppose A and B are persistent, and that x R y in some model N with N(A \rightarrow B, x) = Y. Then in every point reachable from x in N, if the sentence A gets the value Y in that superset, then so does B. Now, since y is itself reachable from x, every point reachable from y is also reachable from x. So in all these points reachable from y, if the sentence A gets the value Y in that superset, then so does B. And that's enough for it to be the case that N(A \rightarrow B, y) = Y.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: That's enough to show that persistence really does hold in these models, no matter what sentence we're looking at.

You can use persistence when you're reasoning about what's possible in these models. For example, let's suppose we have some model N and a point x in N where N(A, x) = Y. Now that we know persistence holds, this is enough to see that N(
eg 
eg A, x) = Y as well. Here's how the reasoning might go:
To show that N(
eg 
eg A, x) = Y, we need to know that for every point y reachable from x in the model N, we have N(
eg A, y) = ?. That in turn means that we need to know that there is some z reachable from y in N with N(A, z) = Y. So what we need to show is this: every point reachable from x in N itself has some point reachable from it in N where A gets the value Y.
But now take any y reachable from x in N. Since A is persistent, we know that N(A, y) = Y. Also, y is reachable from itself. So we've shown what was needed.
Note that this reasoning needed to appeal to persistence to get where it needed to go. This is usual; in reasoning about these models, you will often need to pay attention to persistence.

Okay; those are our models. To get a logic from these, we need to do the usual model-theoretic thing. That is, we need to say which models are counterexamples to which arguments. As you might expect, this will make use of the values assigned by the models at various points.

In fact, it's easiest to say what it takes for a point in a model to be a counterexample to an argument. A model then gets to be a counterexample to an argument by containing a point that's a counterexample to that argument.

A point x in a model M is a counterexample to the argument \Gamma \;/\; A when it meets two conditions:
for every sentence B in \Gamma we have M(B, x) = Y; and
M(A, x) = ?.

This is basically just a designated-value approach again, with Y designated and ? undesignated, except that we need to remember that sentences need both a model and a point in that model to get any value at all.

Before we see some examples, let me remind you: the logic this determines is exactly intuitionistic logic, the same logic determined by the proof system NJ. That is, if an argument has a proof in NJ, then no model provides a counterexample to it; and if an argument has no proof in NJ, then there is a model that provides a counterexample to it. So all the examples of counterexamples will be to arguments that have no proof in NJ, since those are the only arguments that have any counterexamples at all.

Do note that, at least for now, you have to trust me on this: that these models determine the same logic as the proof system NJ. That's certainly not obvious! And we haven't looked at all at why it might be so. But it is indeed so.
You might recall that in intuitionistic logic, the argument \neg \neg p \;/\; p is not valid. So it must have a counterexample. Here is one:
In this model, both points assign ? to \neg p; this is because each of them can reach some point that assigns Y to p. (For both points, this reachable place is 2.)

Because of this, in turn, both points assign Y to 
eg 
eg p; neither of them can reach anywhere in the model that assigns Y to 
eg p. So 1 assigns Y to 
eg 
eg p and ? to p; it is our counterexample, as it occurs in this model.

Here's how you might interpret this: in intuitionistic logic, we might see \neg A as saying something like "I know something that rules out my ever coming to know A". And we might know something that rules out our ever coming to know something that rules out our ever coming to know A, without yet actually knowing A.

Looking at this model, we've got a situation at the point 1 where we don't yet know p, but there's no way in the model to ever come to know 
eg p. So we do know 
eg 
eg p. That means that the argument 
eg 
eg p \;/\; p can have a known premise and an unknown conclusion, so intuitionistic logic judges it invalid.

Here's another example: in intuitionistic logic, the argument from (p \land q) \rightarrow r to (p \rightarrow r) \lor (q \rightarrow r) fails. So it must have a counterexample. Here is one: let the model M be:

Now, think about the values of the sentences in the argument at the point 1 in this model. We have M((p \land q) \rightarrow r, 1) = Y, since the only point reachable from 1 that assigns Y to p \land q in this model is 4, and 4 also assigns Y to r. But both p \rightarrow r and q \rightarrow r get value ? at 1, for p \rightarrow r because of the presence of 2 in the model and for q \rightarrow r because of the presence of 3 in the model. So their disjunction also gets a ? at 1. This gives the premise of this argument getting a Y at 1 in this model, and the conclusion getting a ? there. So we have our counterexample.

This model pictures a certain kind of dependency: if we have both p and q, then we must have r as well, but neither p nor q on its own is enough for r. This is a common kind of dependency: everything from light switches to the law includes situations where two things need to come together to suffice for something else, where neither one would suffice on its own. Our intuitionistic models can picture this very straightforwardly, as this example shows.

However, all of the logics K3, LP, FDE, and classical logic validate this argument. So this seems to be one way in which intuitionistic implication is more flexible than the implications of all those logics.

I'll close these notes by looking at two special kinds of models, models that obey certain extra restrictions. Since models are potential counterexamples, every extra restriction we impose on models removes some potential counterexamples. That means that we'll end up with stronger logics.

All the talk of "stages of development of knowledge" in these models suggests that we might be able to see reachability as a kind of temporal flow within a model, with earlier points being able to reach later points. And you might think that time is totally ordered: that for any distinct times x and y, either x is before y or y is before x.

Our models, though, don't have to reflect this. For example, think of this earlier example model. Points 2 and 3 can each reach the other one, and point 4 can't reach or be reached by anything but itself. There's no great way to understand reachability in this frame as a total temporal order.

We can restrict attention, though, to a special kind of model called total models, where this is required. For a model to be total means that, whenever there are two distinct points x and y in it, then either x R y or y R x. For example, the counterexample I gave above to the argument \neg \neg p / p is a total model.

Suppose we restrict our attention, then, to total models. What logic results? That is, what if we only take a counterexample into account when it is a total model? We won't lose any valid arguments from intuitionistic logic: if an argument has no counterexamples at all, then it certainly has no total counterexamples. That's how we can be sure the resulting logic is stronger than intuitionistic logic. But will we gain any valid arguments? Another way to ask this: are there arguments that do have counterexamples, but that have no total counterexamples?
As it happens, there are. Consider the argument \;/\; (q \rightarrow r) \lor (r \rightarrow q). Here is a counterexample to the argument:
Double-check that this model really is a counterexample. Which point in the model provides the counterexample?

It's 1. The sentence q \rightarrow r gets the value Y at 3, so (q \rightarrow r) \lor (r \rightarrow q) also gets the value Y there. And r \rightarrow q gets the value Y at 2. But at 1, both q \rightarrow r and r \rightarrow q get the value ?; the first because 2 is reachable from 1, and the second because 3 is reachable from 1. So (q \rightarrow r) \lor (r \rightarrow q) also gets the value ? at 1.

But although this argument has a counterexample, it does not have a counterexample that is total. To see this, think what a counterexample to this argument must be like. It must be a model M containing a point 1 where M(q \rightarrow r, 1) = ? and M(r \rightarrow q, 1) = ?. Since M(q \rightarrow r, 1) = ?, there must be some 2 reachable from 1 in M where M(q, 2) = Y but M(r, 2) = ?. And since M(r \rightarrow q, 1) = ?, there must be some 3 reachable from 1 in M where M(r, 3) = Y but M(q, 3) = ?. Think, though, about 2 and 3. Neither of these can be reachable from the other. (Make sure you see why!) So M cannot be total. As such, there are no total counterexamples to this argument.
The logic of total models, then, is properly stronger than intuitionistic logic. It's a logic typically known either as LC or as Gödel-Dummett logic.

We met Dummett in an earlier reading on natural deduction. Kurt Gödel was a 20th century Austrian logician. He is responsible for two of the most important results in 20th century logic: the completeness theorem and the incompleteness theorem. At the outset of WW2 he fled to the US, where he became close friends with Albert Einstein. He later developed a fear of being poisoned, so significant that he eventually refused to eat and starved himself to death.

We can go even farther, though. Suppose we restrict our models yet again, down to models that contain just a single point. In such models, the only point reachable from the point is just that point itself.
All these models are total, so this is really an additional restriction. Again, then, we must end up at a stronger logic, but the question arises: is it properly stronger?

As you can check, the point in a one-point model is basically exactly a Boolean valuation, even for implication and negation. After all, a Boolean valuation assigns \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} to A \rightarrow B unless it assigns \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} to A and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} to B, while our lone point in a model assigns Y to A \rightarrow B unless there's somewhere reachable from it that assigns Y to A and ? to B; if the valuation is itself the only thing around to be reached, these amount to the same thing. The same thing happens with negation. And for conjunction, disjunction, verum, and falsum, they already matched.

This means you can see Boolean valuations as just a special kind of intuitionistic model. Our intuitionistic models are more flexible, since they can contain multiple points, but they do not need to do this, and so Boolean valuations remain available even in this more general setting.

This means that if we restrict our attention to one-point models, we end up with a different logic than intuitionistic logic: we arrive at classical logic. What about LC; is there a difference between classical logic and LC? Indeed there is.
We've already seen a counterexample to \neg \neg p \;/\; p: the model

As you can see, the model is total. So this argument is invalid in LC, just like it's invalid in intuitionistic logic. But it is valid in classical logic. So classical logic is not just stronger than LC; they are distinct logics.

There is a whole field of logic devoted to studying logics in between intuitionistic logic and classical logic in strength. When such logics are reflexive, monotonic, transitive, and substitution invariant, they are known as intermediate logics. The study of intermediate logics, like the study of many other kinds of logics, proceeds by using both proof systems and model systems.

This week, we saw one way to provide models for intuitionistic logic. Each of these models has two components: a frame, and a valuation on that frame. You can think of a frame as a bunch of points with some collection of arrows between them; all that really matters is which of these points are reachable from which. A valuation on a frame assigns some set of atomic formulas to each point in the frame, subject to the condition that when x R y, the set assigned to x must be a subset of the set assigned to y. Given a model, we can calculate a value for every sentence at every point in the model.

A counterexample to an argument is a model containing a point at which all the premises get the value Y but the conclusion gets the value ?. Using this notion of counterexample with these models, we arrive at intuitionistic logic. We have also looked at some stronger logics that we can arrive at by considering certain special kinds of these models.
